summary: Gruber critiques Tom Warren's iPad Pro review for framing iOS 11 through the lens of laptop replacement, arguing that 'your' in the headline should be 'my.' He contends Apple isn't trying to convince everyone to ditch laptops for iPads, and reviews that take this angle miss what makes the iPad genuinely good. He draws a historical analogy to the GUI-vs-command-line debates of the 80s and 90s, which were never settled because the framing was wrong. Gruber predicts the iPad-vs-laptop argument will similarly fade as more people naturally adopt tablet-based workflows.
key_points: Reviews framing iPad Pro as a laptop replacement for 'everyone' fundamentally misunderstand Apple's positioning and the iPad's strengths
Apple executives genuinely believe the Mac has a long future — the iPad isn't meant to kill it
The GUI-vs-command-line debate of the 80s/90s is an instructive analogy: the argument faded not because one side won, but because the framing was wrong
Reviewing an iPad by cataloging what laptops still do better is like reviewing a 1989 Mac SE/30 by focusing on what DOS did faster
The word 'your' vs 'my' captures the entire problem: reviewers project their own workflow needs onto all users
money_quotes: Apple is not trying to convince everyone to replace a traditional Mac or PC with an iPad.
Any review of the iPad and iOS 11 from the perspective of whether it can replace a MacBook for everyone is going to completely miss what is better about the iPad and why.
Apple executives say that the Mac has a bright and long future because they really do think the Mac has a bright and long future.
The argument was never settled, but we don't even use the acronym 'GUI' anymore.
GUIs were better for most people for most tasks and the command line remains for the things it is best at.
We'll just stop talking about it a decade from now, years after many (maybe even most) people are using iPads and iPad-like devices for their computer-based work.
That's what these 'iPad Pro Still Can't Replace Your Laptop' reviews feel like to me.
key_insight: The 'can iPad replace your laptop' framing is as misguided as the GUI-vs-command-line debates of the 80s — the question itself is wrong, and will simply stop being asked as usage patterns evolve.
